tamudigital_local/details: Mixed-media; Mixed-media; M2.D4.F6; Paper is veined with blue pigment; concentric square motif with the letter "K" in the center; apparent medium is pastels; [written in black ink on verso]: "Kay Shelton 7/18/2004"; Section(s) from the Decorated and Decorative Paper Terms Thesaurus: V; Signed; Not stamped; Not stickered; Not embossed; This is a form of paper decoration invented by Mindy Dubansky. Crayons are used to cover the sheet with their wax in various colors. The sheet is then soaked in water and crumpled up creating cracks in the wax.  Then a colored pigment is brushed onto the sheet filling the cracks. The sheet is then ironed against a backing sheet to flatten it out. In one case, the backing sheet picked up the pattern that was ironed onto it and is part of this two sheet set.

tamudigital_local/details: Surface application; Marbled; M4.D4.F5; Leaf-like stenciled figures layered over top of hair vein marbling pattern; pencil lines appear on recto indicating where marbler intended to cut the larger sheet into 4 smaller sheets; 4 layers of marbling; [stamped in purple ink on verso]: "KARLI FRIGGE LOCHEMSEWEG 75 7215 RA JOPPE NEDERLAND"; Section(s) from the Decorated and Decorative Paper Terms Thesaurus: VIII.F.3; Not signed; Stamped; Not stickered; Not embossed; Because of some of her marbling pigments were spoiled by May flies, Karli was unable to use these pigments for her regular marbling.  Not to waste them, she developed a kind of marbling she called "Plants," unlike anything she (or anyone else) has ever done before or since.  She was able to get a small number of marbled sheets from these pigments before she ran out of them.  One of the extraordinary features of these papers, made possible because of the texture of the pigments, was that Karli could marble one pattern over another in such a way that all layers of marbling are visible.
